Two Pointers.trigger: Pairs summing to target in a sorted array, Palindrome check, Remove duplicates in-place, Maximize area/span between two boundaries (greedy: move the shorter side)
Two Pointers.gotchas: Array MUST be sorted before applying two pointers. For 3Sum, after fixing index i, always skip duplicate values with `if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i-1]) continue;`. Also skip duplicates for l and r after finding a valid triplet to avoid duplicate results. For LC 11, the greedy rule is: always move the SHORTER side — moving the taller side can only shrink width while height is still capped by the shorter side, so it cannot improve the area.
problems: LC 167. Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

Sliding Window.trigger: Longest/Shortest subarray or substring matching a condition, At most K distinct elements, Minimum window containing all characters
Sliding Window.complexity: Time: O(N) | Space: O(1) or O(K)
Sliding Window.gotchas: Track what makes a window 'invalid' carefully — the shrink condition is the key insight. For character problems, use `unordered_map<char,int>` or `int freq[26]` as state. Always update state incrementally (never rescan). For minimum-window problems, shrink when valid (not when invalid).
problems: LC 3. Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

Binary Search (Sorted Array).trigger: Search for a target in a sorted array, Find leftmost/rightmost occurrence via lower_bound / upper_bound, Search through a rotation break in a sorted sequence
Binary Search (Sorted Array).gotchas: `lower_bound` returns the first element >= target, NOT necessarily == target. Always check `*it == target`. Use a half-open interval `[left, right)`: set `right = nums.size()` and narrow with `right = mid` (not `mid - 1`). For rotated arrays, first determine which half is sorted (compare `nums[left]` with `nums[mid]`), then check if target lies in that sorted half.
problems: LC 34. Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
LC 33. Search in Rotated Sorted Array

Binary Search (Answer Space).trigger: Maximize the minimum, Minimize the maximum, Feasibility check over a monotonic answer space (e.g., 'Can we achieve X?'). Use when searching ON VALUES, not indices.
Binary Search (Answer Space).complexity: Time: O(N log(Max - Min)) | Space: O(1)
Binary Search (Answer Space).gotchas: INFINITE LOOP risk: when seeking the rightmost boundary, if you use `left = mid` without rounding up mid, then `mid = left` when left+1==right, causing an infinite loop. Always use `mid = left + (right-left+1)/2` for the rightmost pattern. Verify: does `isValid` monotonically transition false→true or true→false?
problems: LC 1011. Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days

Prefix Sum.trigger: O(1) range sum after O(N) preprocessing, Count subarrays summing to a target, Cumulative property queries over arbitrary ranges
Prefix Sum.complexity: Time: O(N) build, O(1) query | Space: O(N)
Prefix Sum.gotchas: Use 1-indexed prefix array: `prefix[i]` = sum of first i elements, so `prefix[0] = 0`. Range sum [l, r] = `prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]`. For the hashmap variant, ALWAYS initialize `freq[0] = 1` before the loop to correctly count subarrays that start at index 0. For negative numbers, `unordered_map` still works correctly.
problems: LC 238. Product of Array Except Self

HashMap (Complement / Grouping).trigger: Find pairs or complements in O(N), Group elements by a shared key property, Frequency counting, Longest consecutive run
HashMap (Complement / Grouping).gotchas: For Two Sum: insert the current element into the map AFTER checking for its complement — this prevents using the same element twice (e.g., target=6, nums=[3]). Prefer `unordered_map` (O(1) avg) over `map` (O(log N)). For consecutive sequence, start the chain only when `num-1` is absent to avoid O(N²) inner loops.

Monotonic Deque.trigger: Maximum or minimum in every sliding window of size K, Subarray with constraint on range (max - min)
Monotonic Deque.gotchas: Store INDICES in the deque, not values — needed to check if front is outside the window. Step order matters: (1) remove expired front, (2) remove smaller back, (3) push current, (4) record result. Use `<=` when removing from back for max-deque to handle duplicates correctly.
problems: LC 862. Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K
LC 1438. Longest Continuous Subarray With Absolute Diff Less Than or Equal to Limit

Sweep Line (Intervals).trigger: Count maximum concurrent overlapping intervals, Merge adjacent or overlapping ranges, Track active events across a continuous timeline
Sweep Line (Intervals).complexity: Time: O(N log N) | Space: O(N)
Sweep Line (Intervals).gotchas: Be careful about boundary inclusiveness: if [1,2] and [2,3] overlap (closed intervals), decrement at `interval[1]+1`. If open intervals don't overlap, decrement at `interval[1]`. Use `map<int,int>` (sorted by key), NOT `unordered_map`.

Quick Select (nth_element).trigger: Kth largest or smallest element in an unsorted static array, Find top-K elements without a full sort, Median of a static collection
Quick Select (nth_element).complexity: Time: O(N) average, O(N²) worst | Space: O(1)
Quick Select (nth_element).gotchas: ALWAYS use a RANDOM pivot to prevent O(N²) worst case on sorted/adversarial inputs. Use Quick Select for static arrays; use a heap for streaming data. The 3-way partition handles arrays with many duplicates efficiently and avoids TLE.
problems: LC 462. Minimum Moves to Equal Array Elements II
LC 973. K Closest Points to Origin

Monotonic Stack (NGE / Histogram).trigger: Next greater or smaller element at each index, Maximum area enclosed within a bounded histogram, Extend histogram area problem to 2D grids (Maximal Rectangle)
Monotonic Stack (NGE / Histogram).gotchas: Store INDICES (not values) — you almost always need to compute distances. Decide stack type: Decreasing stack (top=smallest) → 'Next Greater Element'; Increasing stack (top=largest) → 'Next Smaller Element'. After the loop, elements still in the stack have no next greater/smaller element.

Expression Parsing.trigger: Validate bracket or parenthesis matching, Evaluate expressions with nested parentheses and operators, Decode nested encoded sequences
Expression Parsing.gotchas: For multi-digit numbers, accumulate with `num = num * 10 + (c - '0')` — don't process each digit independently. When encountering '(', push the CURRENT result and sign onto the stack, then reset. When encountering ')', flush the current number first before restoring. Always check `!st.empty()` before popping.

Priority Queue (Heap).trigger: Maintain the top-K elements dynamically, Kth largest or smallest from a data stream, Running median, Merge multiple sorted sequences
Priority Queue (Heap).complexity: Time: O(N log K) | Space: O(K)
Priority Queue (Heap).gotchas: C++ max-heap is default; for min-heap use `greater<>` as the third template argument. In a custom comparator struct, `return a > b` means 'a has lower priority' (min-heap behavior). For Top K Largest: use a MIN-heap of size K and pop when oversized — the top is the Kth largest. Don't confuse with 'Top K Smallest' (use max-heap).
problems: LC 215. Kth Largest Element in an Array
LC 295. Find Median from Data Stream

Linked List (Reverse / Fast-Slow).trigger: Reverse a linked list in-place, Find the middle node with fast/slow pointers, Detect and locate a cycle (Floyd's algorithm)
Linked List (Reverse / Fast-Slow).gotchas: ALWAYS save `curr->next` before overwriting `curr->next = prev` in reversal. For cycle start detection: after fast and slow meet inside the cycle, reset ONE pointer to head and advance BOTH one step at a time — they meet exactly at the cycle start. For finding the middle, slow stops at the first middle node for odd-length lists.

Tree Traversal (BFS / DFS).trigger: Level-by-level traversal, Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA), Tree height and diameter, Root-to-leaf path conditions
Tree Traversal (BFS / DFS).complexity: Time: O(N) | Space: O(H) DFS, O(N) BFS
Tree Traversal (BFS / DFS).gotchas: For BFS level order: snapshot `q.size()` at the START of each level loop — the size changes as you enqueue children. For DFS, always handle the `nullptr` base case. For LCA: if BOTH left and right return non-null, current root IS the LCA.
problems: LC 102. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
LC 104. Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
LC 236. Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree

Trie (Prefix Tree).trigger: Fast prefix matching and autocomplete, Simultaneous multi-word search in a grid, Bitwise XOR maximization over a set
Trie (Prefix Tree).complexity: Time: O(L) per operation | Space: O(N * L * 26)
Trie (Prefix Tree).gotchas: `search()` requires `isEnd == true`; `startsWith()` does NOT. Raw pointer Trie MUST have a destructor to avoid memory leaks. For Word Search II, store the matched word string inside the TrieNode to avoid re-tracing the path. Consider using `unordered_map<char, TrieNode*>` for sparse alphabets.
problems: LC 211. Design Add and Search Words Data Structure

Tree (Serialize / Reconstruct).trigger: Serialize and reconstruct a binary tree using null markers, Rebuild a tree from two complementary traversal sequences
Tree (Serialize / Reconstruct).gotchas: Preorder alone CANNOT reconstruct a tree — you need either inorder OR null markers. With null markers, preorder is sufficient. For construction from preorder+inorder: preorder[0] is always the root; its position in inorder splits left/right subtrees. Use a hashmap for O(1) inorder lookup, avoiding O(N²) complexity.
problems: LC 297. Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
LC 105. Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
LC 106. Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal

Backtracking (Perm / Comb / Subset).trigger: Generate all permutations, combinations, or subsets, Constraint-satisfying exhaustive search (e.g., N-Queens), Explore all valid configurations with pruning
Backtracking (Perm / Comb / Subset).complexity: Time: O(N!) permutations, O(2^N) subsets | Space: O(N)
Backtracking (Perm / Comb / Subset).gotchas: Two distinct patterns: (1) Combinations use a `start` index to prevent revisiting. (2) Permutations use a `visited[]` array (all positions are candidates each time). For permutation deduplication: sort first, then `if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i-1] && !visited[i-1]) continue` — the `!visited[i-1]` condition is subtle but critical.

BFS (Shortest Path).trigger: Shortest path in an unweighted graph, Minimum transformations between states, Bipartite graph verification
BFS (Shortest Path).gotchas: Mark nodes as visited WHEN ENQUEUING (not when dequeuing). Dequeuing-based marking allows duplicate entries in the queue, causing TLE or wrong shortest path counts. BFS on unweighted graphs ALWAYS gives the optimal (shortest) path — use Dijkstra only for weighted graphs.
problems: LC 863. All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree
LC 1091. Shortest Path in Binary Matrix

DFS (Cycle Detection).trigger: Detect cycle in directed graph, Validate DAG, Find safe nodes (nodes that don't lead to a cycle), DFS-based connected components
DFS (Cycle Detection).gotchas: A cycle exists in a directed graph only if you reach a GRAY node (in the current DFS path). Reaching a BLACK node is fine (it's already fully processed). For undirected graphs, you MUST track the parent node to avoid false positives from the bi-directional edge you arrived from.

Grid (DFS / Multi-Source BFS).trigger: Count or flood-fill connected regions in a matrix, Shortest path through a grid, Multi-source BFS (enqueue all sources simultaneously)
Grid (DFS / Multi-Source BFS).gotchas: Always check BOTH bounds AND visited status before enqueuing or recursing. In-place modification (`grid[r][c] = '0'`) avoids a separate `visited` matrix. For multi-source BFS (e.g., Rotting Oranges), enqueue ALL sources BEFORE starting BFS — don't process them sequentially. Mark visited when enqueuing (not dequeuing).

Topological Sort (Kahn's).trigger: Order tasks with prerequisites, Detect impossible scheduling cycles, Reconstruct a valid execution sequence from dependency rules
Topological Sort (Kahn's).complexity: Time: O(V + E) | Space: O(V + E)
Topological Sort (Kahn's).gotchas: If `order.size() != numCourses`, a cycle was detected — topological sort is undefined for cyclic graphs. Build BOTH the `graph` adjacency list AND the `indegree` array from prerequisites — a common mistake is building only one. The edge direction matters: `edge[1] -> edge[0]` means 'edge[1] is a prerequisite of edge[0]'.

Union-Find (DSU).trigger: Dynamic connectivity, Cycle detection in undirected graphs, Group/Set union and membership, Minimum Spanning Tree (Kruskal)
Union-Find (DSU).complexity: Time: O(α(V)) ≈ O(1) amortized | Space: O(V)
Union-Find (DSU).gotchas: The return value of `unite()` (whether a merge happened) is crucial: if `unite()` returns false for a new edge in an undirected graph, a CYCLE was detected. Use Union by Size (or Rank) to keep trees flat — without it, DSU degrades to O(log N). Path halving (iterative) is preferred over recursive path compression for stack safety.
problems: LC 323. Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
LC 1584. Min Cost to Connect All Points

Dijkstra.trigger: Shortest path in a weighted graph with non-negative edges, Minimum cost to reach all nodes from a source, Implicit state graph (e.g., minimum cost with constraints on steps or fuel)
Dijkstra.complexity: Time: O((V + E) log V) | Space: O(V + E)
Dijkstra.gotchas: Dijkstra does NOT work with NEGATIVE edge weights (use Bellman-Ford instead). The `if (d > dist[u]) continue;` 'lazy deletion' check is critical — without it, stale priority queue entries cause incorrect results. Initialize `dist` to INT_MAX and check for overflow when computing `dist[u] + weight`.
problems: LC 787. Cheapest Flights Within K Stops

Greedy (Interval / Sort-by-End).trigger: Interval scheduling (maximize non-overlapping intervals), Minimum jumps or steps to reach the end, Sort by a greedy criterion (e.g., earliest end time) and pick greedily
Greedy (Interval / Sort-by-End).complexity: Time: O(N log N) for sorting | Space: O(1)
Greedy (Interval / Sort-by-End).gotchas: For interval scheduling, sort by END time (not start time). Sorting by start time is incorrect and a common mistake. Always ask yourself: 'Can a counterexample disprove this greedy?' Greedy is NOT always applicable — if in doubt, try DP first.

Linear DP (1D / LIS).trigger: Optimal decision at each step depends on a fixed number of prior states, Binary choice (take or skip) at each element, Longest strictly increasing subsequence
Linear DP (1D / LIS).complexity: Time: O(N) or O(N log N) for LIS | Space: O(1) or O(N)
Linear DP (1D / LIS).gotchas: Space optimization: if dp[i] depends only on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], use two variables. For LIS O(N log N): `tails` is NOT the actual LIS — it stores the smallest tails and is used only for length calculation. Use `lower_bound` for strictly increasing, `upper_bound` for non-decreasing. Always verify base cases (dp[0] and dp[1]).

Knapsack DP (0/1 vs Unbounded).trigger: Select items to hit an exact target sum, Maximize value under a capacity constraint, Partition a set into equal halves, Coin combinations with or without item reuse
Knapsack DP (0/1 vs Unbounded).gotchas: The CRITICAL rule: 0/1 Knapsack → iterate weight BACKWARDS (prevent reuse). Unbounded Knapsack → iterate weight FORWARDS (allow reuse). Getting this backwards is the #1 mistake. For partition problems: if total sum is odd, return false immediately. Initialize `dp[0] = true` (can always form sum 0).

Grid DP (Unique Paths / Min Cost).trigger: Count or optimize paths across a 2D grid, Minimum cost movement with restricted directional choices, State requires both row and column to be fully defined, Handle obstacle cells that block movement
Grid DP (Unique Paths / Min Cost).complexity: Time: O(M*N) | Space: O(M*N) reducible to O(N)
Grid DP (Unique Paths / Min Cost).gotchas: Initialize the ENTIRE first row and first column as base cases before the inner loop. For obstacles: set `dp[r][c] = 0` (for count) or `INT_MAX` (for min cost) at obstacle cells. Space can be reduced to O(N) by maintaining a single row array and processing in-place — process row by row, left to right.

String DP (LCS / Edit Distance).trigger: Transform one string into another with minimum operations, Longest Common Subsequence (LCS), Longest palindromic subsequence or substring
String DP (LCS / Edit Distance).complexity: Time: O(M*N) | Space: O(M*N) reducible to O(N)
String DP (LCS / Edit Distance).gotchas: LCS base case: `dp[i][0] = dp[0][j] = 0` (empty string has LCS 0 with anything). Edit Distance base cases: `dp[i][0] = i` (delete i chars) and `dp[0][j] = j` (insert j chars). Space can be reduced to O(N) using two 1D arrays (previous and current row). Note: LCS gives length only; backtracking through dp table recovers the actual sequence.

Interval DP (dp[l][r]).trigger: Optimal value over all contiguous sub-intervals, Break or merge array segments optimally, Two-player alternating game on a linear structure
Interval DP (dp[l][r]).gotchas: ALWAYS iterate by LENGTH of interval (outer loop), not by start index. The split point k represents the LAST operation performed — this is the key insight for Burst Balloons. Memoize with `dp[l][r]`. Base cases: `dp[i][i]` for single-element intervals. Adding sentinel values at array boundaries (e.g., nums[-1] = nums[n] = 1 for Burst Balloons) simplifies boundary handling.
problems: LC 1312. Minimum Insertion Steps to Make a String Palindrome

Digit DP.trigger: Count integers in range [L, R] satisfying digit-based constraints (e.g., no digit 4, digit sum <= K, non-decreasing digits)
Digit DP.gotchas: Note: Digit DP is a competitive programming pattern. The `limit` flag prevents placing a digit larger than the corresponding digit in the bound. The `lead` flag handles leading zeros (e.g., '007' is just 7). Always use `memset(memo, -1, sizeof(memo))` before each top-level call.
problems: LC 902. Numbers At Most N Given Digit Set

Bitmask DP (2^N State).trigger: N <= 20, TSP (Traveling Salesman), Enumerate all subsets/permutations of a small set, State represented as a bitmask
Bitmask DP (2^N State).complexity: Time: O(2^N * N²) | Space: O(2^N * N)
Bitmask DP (2^N State).gotchas: Note: Bitmask DP is a competitive programming pattern. Only practical for N <= 20 (2^20 ≈ 1M states). Watch for INT_MAX overflow: check `dp[mask][u] != INT_MAX` before adding to it. When enumerating subsets with `sub = (sub-1) & mask`, the loop must use `sub > 0` (not `sub != 0`) to include the empty subset separately.
problems: LC 847. Shortest Path Visiting All Nodes

Bit Manipulation.trigger: Find unique element (XOR), Count set bits, Check power of two, Toggle/flip without extra space, Enumerate subsets
Bit Manipulation.complexity: Time: O(N) or O(1) | Space: O(1)
Bit Manipulation.gotchas: `n & (n-1)` clears the lowest set bit (0 if n is a power of 2). XOR is self-inverse: `a ^ a = 0` and `a ^ 0 = a`. Beware of signed integer overflow with left shift on int: use `1LL << i` for i >= 31. Use `__builtin_popcount(n)` for fast bit counting in competitive/interview settings.

Modulo Inverse & Comb(n, k).trigger: Count distinct arrangements or selections modulo a prime, Compute nCr efficiently under modular arithmetic
Modulo Inverse & Comb(n, k).complexity: Time: O(N log MOD) prep, O(1) per query | Space: O(N)
Modulo Inverse & Comb(n, k).gotchas: Fermat's Little Theorem only works when MOD is PRIME and n is not 0 mod p. Precompute `fact[]` AND `inv_fact[]` (precomputed inverses of factorials) for O(1) per nCr query — computing `modInverse` on the fly is O(log MOD) per call. When multiplying two `long long` values close to MOD (≈1e9), the product can overflow: always take `% MOD` after each multiplication.
problems: LC 1569. Number of Ways to Reorder Array to Get Same BST

Rolling Hash (Rabin-Karp).trigger: O(N) substring search and hashing, Detect repeated substrings, Avoid TLE from naive O(N²) string comparison
Rolling Hash (Rabin-Karp).gotchas: CRITICAL: `h1 - h2` can be NEGATIVE in C++ (unlike Python). Always add MOD before taking the final modulo: `(h1 - h2 + MOD) % MOD`. Choose a prime BASE (e.g., 131) and a large prime MOD to reduce hash collisions. For extra safety, use double hashing (two different MOD/BASE pairs) to virtually eliminate false positives.

Segment Tree (Range Query).trigger: Dynamic range queries (sum/max/min) with frequent point or range updates, Need O(log N) per both query AND update (prefix sum cannot update in O(log N)), Range updates with lazy propagation
Segment Tree (Range Query).complexity: Time: O(log N) per query/update | Space: O(N)
Segment Tree (Range Query).gotchas: Always allocate `4 * N` space for the tree array. Left child = `2*node`, right child = `2*node+1` (1-indexed node). The INVALID sentinel must be the identity element for your operation (INVALID=-1 for max, 0 for sum, INT_MAX for min). For range updates, you need an additional `lazy[]` propagation array — significantly more complex.
problems: LC 315. Count of Smaller Numbers After Self

Fenwick Tree (BIT).trigger: Prefix sum with frequent point updates, Count inversions, Simpler alternative to Segment Tree for invertible operations
Fenwick Tree (BIT).complexity: Time: O(log N) per update/query | Space: O(N)
Fenwick Tree (BIT).gotchas: BIT is ALWAYS 1-indexed — offset your input indices by +1. `i & (-i)` isolates the lowest set bit (the key operation). `update`: add `i & (-i)` to move forward. `query`: subtract `i & (-i)` to move to parent. BIT only supports operations with an INVERSE (sum, XOR) — NOT max/min (no inverse). For max queries, use a Segment Tree instead.
problems: LC 315. Count of Smaller Numbers After Self

LRU Cache.trigger: Design a fixed-capacity key-value cache with O(1) get and put, Least Recently Used eviction
LRU Cache.complexity: Time: O(1) get and put | Space: O(capacity)
LRU Cache.gotchas: Use a DOUBLY linked list (not singly) so you can remove any node in O(1). `std::list::splice()` moves a node to a new position in O(1) without memory allocation. The hashmap stores list ITERATORS (not values) for O(1) access. When evicting, erase from the hashmap BEFORE popping from the list to avoid dangling iterator access.
